question: An endpoint takes 200 ms: 5 ms of CPU and 195 ms blocked on a database and an HTTP call. Handled one request at a time on a 16-core machine, it measures 4.96 req/s at 2.51% of one core. What is the bottleneck?
options: The CPU — 4.96 req/s means the processor is saturated, so a machine with a higher clock speed and more cores will roughly double throughput
The 195 ms of downstream latency — until the database and the payments API get faster, no change on your side can improve throughput

The structure of the code, not any hardware resource. Nothing is saturated: the CPU is 0.157% busy across 16 cores. The server does 4.96 req/s only because request 2 is not allowed to start while request 1 is blocked. Overlapping six requests moved the same work to 27.89 req/s with essentially unchanged CPU usage
Memory bandwidth — 200 ms per request implies the working set is spilling out of cache into main memory on every call
explanation: Nothing here is saturated, which is exactly what makes the diagnosis counter-intuitive. Utilization near zero on every resource means the limit is structural: the program can only have one request in flight at a time, so throughput is pinned at 1/latency. The tempting answer is 'buy a faster box', and it produces 4.96 req/s on the faster box too, because the CPU was never the constraint.

question: A configuration with 20 workers and a 40 ms service time sustains 500 req/s. Its measured unloaded latency is 39.7 ms, so 1/W is 25.2/s. Why is 1/W wrong by a factor of 20?
options: Because throughput is concurrency divided by latency, X = L/W, not 1/W. The 20 workers each retire one 40 ms request at a time, so the system retires 20 requests every 40 ms. 1/W is only the throughput of a system whose concurrency happens to be exactly 1 — the missing factor is always L, and this is Little's Law rearranged
Because the 39.7 ms measurement includes queueing time, and the true service time is 20x smaller than measured
Because throughput and latency are measured over different windows, so the two numbers can never be compared directly and no relationship exists between them
Because 500 req/s is the burst capacity, while 1/W correctly gives the sustained rate the system can hold indefinitely
explanation: This is the single most common arithmetic error in performance discussions. Latency describes one request; throughput describes the system; the bridge between them is how many requests are in flight, which is exactly what Little's Law supplies. The factor by which 1/W is wrong is always the concurrency — here 20, the worker count — and that is why a system can improve throughput and worsen latency in the same change.

question: Your service handles 800 req/s with a mean latency of 250 ms, and is configured with a 50-thread pool. What does Little's Law tell you, and what should the pool be?
options: The pool is fine: 50 threads on a machine that only needs 4.0 cores of CPU is already generous, and adding threads will only increase context-switching overhead
L = 800 x 0.250 = 200 requests are in flight, but the pool is 50, so nothing is wrong — Little's Law describes arrivals, not the pool, and the extra 150 are served by the kernel
You cannot compute anything without knowing the service time and the arrival distribution, since Little's Law only holds for M/M/1 queues with Poisson arrivals

L = 800 x 0.250 = 200 requests must be in flight, so a 50-thread pool physically caps you at 50/0.250 = 200 req/s and the other 600 req/s are queueing or being refused. Size it at L x headroom = 200 x 1.5 = 300 slots — and note that is a completely separate calculation from the 4.0 cores of CPU the same load needs
explanation: Little's Law makes no assumption about arrival or service distributions or queueing discipline — it is an accounting identity that holds in any stable system, which is why the third option is wrong in an appealing-sounding way. The practical consequence is that pool size and core count are two independent sizing problems: threads exist to hold waiting requests, cores exist to execute work, and 'one worker per core' is folklore inherited from CPU-bound batch jobs.

question: A dashboard shows your bottleneck resource at 80% utilization and someone says 'we have 20% headroom'. Using W = S/(1-rho), which the lesson confirmed by simulation to within 2%, what is actually true?
options: They are right about the arithmetic but wrong about the risk: at 80% the latency multiplier is only 1.25x, and the danger is purely that a spike could exceed 100%

At 80% busy you are already paying a 5x latency multiplier, and the next step to 90% is only +12.5% more work but +100% more latency. Going 90% to 99% is +10% work for +900% latency. Utilization is a latency multiplier, not a fuel gauge, and it is non-linear precisely where the dashboard still looks calm
The formula does not apply to real services because arrivals are never exactly Poisson, so utilization can safely be pushed to 95% on any system with more than one server
It depends entirely on the absolute service time: with a fast enough S, 99% utilization produces no user-visible latency increase at all
explanation: The (1 - rho) denominator goes to zero, so the last few percent of a resource are the most expensive thing you can buy — and they are usually bought accidentally, by a traffic spike, a retry storm, or a deploy that removes half your pods for thirty seconds. Variability does change the exact multiplier (regular arrivals queue less, bursty ones queue more, and more servers flatten the curve), but the knee always exists and is always well below 100%.

question: You scale a service from 16 workers to 1,024 and measured throughput falls from roughly 9x to 6.5x baseline. Amdahl's Law with the same serial fraction predicts 19.6x and never decreases. What is happening?
options: Amdahl's Law is being applied wrongly: with 1,024 workers the serial fraction must be recomputed per worker, and the corrected ceiling explains the decline
The workers are CPU-starved — 1,024 workers on fewer physical cores means each gets a smaller time slice, and the fix is more machines
Measurement error: throughput cannot decrease as workers are added, so the load generator has become the bottleneck and is not offering enough load

The Universal Scalability Law's coherency term, kappa x N x (N-1). Amdahl only models contention (serialized work), which flattens the curve but never bends it down. Coherency — workers agreeing with each other via cache-line invalidation, lock handoffs, a hot row, a distributed lock — grows quadratically and eventually beats the linear gain. The lesson's curve peaked at 97 workers, matching the closed form sqrt((1-sigma)/kappa) = 97.5
explanation: Throughput that falls as capacity rises is the unmistakable signature of coherency cost, and it is diagnostic: no amount of additional capacity fixes it, and adding more usually makes an incident worse. Amdahl's curve is monotonic, so it can never produce that shape, which is why the USL exists. The remedy is to find the shared thing every worker touches — a hot row, a leader, a lock, a cache everyone invalidates — rather than to keep scaling out.

question: Two endpoints each take 200 ms. Endpoint A spends 195 ms blocked on a database; endpoint B spends 190 ms hashing a password with bcrypt. You are asked to add async I/O to both. What happens?
options: A improves a lot and B improves essentially not at all. A is I/O-bound (CPU time / wall time near 0.025) so its 195 ms of idle time can be filled with other requests — concurrency, a structuring property that works even on one core. B is CPU-bound (ratio near 1.0): there is no idle time to reclaim, so async adds only queueing, and the only real fixes are more cores (parallelism, via processes) or fewer instructions
Both improve by roughly the same factor, because async I/O lets the event loop interleave any two operations regardless of whether they are waiting or computing
Neither improves, because a single-threaded event loop can never exceed the throughput of a single blocking thread — you need multiple processes in both cases
B improves more than A, because CPU-bound work benefits most from the reduced per-request memory overhead of coroutines compared with OS threads
explanation: Concurrency (dealing with many things at once, a property of program structure) and parallelism (doing many things at once, a property of execution) are orthogonal, and the classification that tells you which one you need is CPU-bound versus I/O-bound. The diagnostic is one ratio: CPU time over wall time per request, near 1.0 for CPU-bound and near 0 for I/O-bound. Adding threads or coroutines to saturated CPU buys nothing and adds queue; adding processes to idle-waiting work costs memory and buys nothing.
